title: Benin’s New President Seeks New Outreach to Togo and Niger | African Arguments
gist: New Beninese president Romuald Wadagni, Talon's hand-picked technocratic successor elected with no real opposition, is prioritising outreach to Niger and Togo - driven by Talon's business networks, the suspended Niger-Benin oil pipeline, and fiscal-centralisation reforms - to revive economic ties strained by Benin's 2023 ECOWAS sanctions on Niger and port rivalry with Togo.

insights.claim: Romuald Wadagni won Benin's presidency facing only one opponent who took under 6% of the vote, after the main opposition Les Democrates were shut out by ballot-access endorsement requirements and lost all parliamentary seats.
insights.speaker: Alec Soltes
insights.so_what: Signals a managed, low-competition political system; legitimacy is sought through performance and diplomacy rather than the ballot.
insights.quote: won less that 6% of the vote against Wadagni.

insights.claim: Wadagni is pursuing his own source of legitimacy via a West African diplomatic tour (Niger, Nigeria, Togo, Burkina Faso, Côte d'Ivoire) because his mandate rests on technocratic competence rather than an independent political base.
insights.speaker: Alec Soltes
insights.so_what: Regional diplomacy doubles as domestic legitimacy theatre, predicting a foreign-policy-active early term.
insights.locator: Given this, Wadagni is likely pursuing

insights.claim: Talon chose the technocrat Wadagni partly because, as finance minister, he had fewer opportunities to build an independent support base, making him dependent on Talon's political and financial networks.
insights.speaker: Alec Soltes
insights.so_what: Dependence structures Wadagni's policy priorities, including which regional relationships he prioritises.
insights.locator: One of the likely reasons Talon chose Wadagni

insights.claim: Talon secured his political future by installing a dependent successor, reducing his risk of investigation or prosecution after his 2012 embezzlement accusation and flight to France.
insights.speaker: Alec Soltes
insights.so_what: Explains continuity incentives that will keep Talon-era policies in place.

insights.claim: The Niger-Benin oil pipeline, suspended in 2025, is one of Benin's most consequential infrastructure projects and a central motive for Wadagni's outreach to Niamey.
insights.speaker: Alec Soltes
insights.so_what: Reviving the pipeline is a concrete, trackable asset-level catalyst for Benin-Niger relations.
insights.quote: the Niger-Benin oil pipeline is one of the most consequential infrastructure projects in Benin

insights.claim: Benin-Niger relations soured when Talon joined ECOWAS's 2023 sanctions and border closure against Niger after the coup, culminating in the halting of the pipeline deal and hitting Benin's economy hard.
insights.speaker: Alec Soltes
insights.so_what: Shows the cost of the ECOWAS rupture, and why Benin now seeks a reset given Niger's 2025 ECOWAS exit.

insights.claim: Wadagni oversaw a 40% increase in Benin's GDP per capita between 2016 and 2024 as finance minister, the economic record underpinning his electoral position.
insights.speaker: Alec Soltes
insights.so_what: Quantifies the growth track record that anchors the regime's performance-based legitimacy.
insights.quote: Wadagni oversaw a 40% increase in GDP per capita between 2016 and 2024.
insights.locator: First, it allowed for regime continuity

insights.claim: Talon's fiscal centralisation - consolidating tax collection, procurement and state-owned enterprises - built state capacity that positions Benin to capitalise on deeper economic ties with Niger and Togo.
insights.speaker: Alec Soltes
insights.so_what: Institutional capacity reinforces Benin's policy effectiveness and trade-integration options.

insights.claim: Benin and Togo maintain a relationship of 'polite coldness' driven by competing economic interests, notably rivalry between the ports of Lomé and Cotonou.
insights.speaker: Alec Soltes
insights.so_what: Port competition is the structural friction Wadagni must navigate to reset Togo relations.
insights.quote: analysts had described their relationship as “polite coldness.”
